qa_pairs.question: What are the two main sections of the job submission page in Open OnDemand?
qa_pairs.answer: The job submission page is split between the job parameters on the left and the job script itself on the right.

qa_pairs.question: What aspects of a job can users control using the job parameters on the submission page?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can control resources such as the number of nodes, number of tasks per node, wall clock time, and the output file name.

qa_pairs.question: Will changes made to job parameters automatically update the job script?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, any changes made to the job parameters will be reflected in the job script automatically.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of the 'Script Location' field on the job submission page?
qa_pairs.answer: The 'Script Location' specifies the directory where the job script will be saved and where your job will be run from.

qa_pairs.question: How do you specify the name of the job script file?
qa_pairs.answer: You specify the name of the job script file using the 'Script Name' field.

qa_pairs.question: Which field determines the name of the job as it appears in the job queue?
qa_pairs.answer: The 'Job Name' field specifies the name of the job that will appear in the job queue.

qa_pairs.question: Can I choose which cluster to submit my job to from the job submission page?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, the 'Cluster' field allows you to change which cluster to submit your job to, such as Trillium (default) or Trillium-GPU.

qa_pairs.question: What additional parameter becomes available if 'Trillium-GPU' is selected as the cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: Selecting 'Trillium-GPU' will provide an additional job parameter to request GPU resources.

qa_pairs.question: How do I submit a job after configuring the script and parameters?
qa_pairs.answer: Once satisfied with the job script, click on the 'Submit' button to submit the job to the scheduler and save your script to the 'Script Location'.

qa_pairs.question: What happens after a job is successfully submitted?
qa_pairs.answer: If your job was submitted successfully, you will see a confirmation message at the top of the page with your job ID.

qa_pairs.question: Are the template scripts in Open Composer ready for immediate use without modification?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the template scripts provided in Open Composer are basic examples to get you started, and you will need to modify them further to suit your specific needs.

qa_pairs.question: What types of modifications might be necessary for a job script?
qa_pairs.answer: Modifications might include loading required modules and specifying input/output files.

qa_pairs.question: What limits must job scripts adhere to?
qa_pairs.answer: The job script still needs to conform to the limits set by the Trillium Slurm scheduler.

qa_pairs.question: Where can users find more information on how to write job scripts?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should refer to the Trillium documentation for more information on how to write job scripts.

qa_pairs.question: How do I monitor submitted jobs within Open Composer?
qa_pairs.answer: To monitor submitted jobs in Open Composer, navigate to the 'History' tab.

qa_pairs.question: What information does the 'History' tab display for submitted jobs?
qa_pairs.answer: The 'History' tab displays a list of all submitted jobs along with their status, such as Queued, Running, Completed, or Failed.

qa_pairs.question: How can users filter jobs in the 'History' tab?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can filter jobs by using the 'Filter' text box at the top right or by using the checkboxes below.

qa_pairs.question: What information is provided by clicking on the 'Job ID' in the 'History' tab?
qa_pairs.answer: Clicking on the 'Job ID' opens the job in my.SciNet, which displays performance statistics and more detailed Slurm information about the job.

qa_pairs.question: What does clicking on 'Application' in the 'History' tab do?
qa_pairs.answer: Clicking on 'Application' opens the job script editor of the template that was used.

qa_pairs.question: How can I access the directory where my job script is located from the 'History' tab?
qa_pairs.answer: Clicking on 'Script Location' opens an OOD file browser window at the location of the job script. A small terminal icon there will also open a terminal in that location.

qa_pairs.question: What is shown when clicking on 'Script Name' in the 'History' tab?
qa_pairs.answer: Clicking on 'Script Name' displays the job script that was submitted to the scheduler.

qa_pairs.question: How can I resubmit or modify a previously run job in Open Composer?
qa_pairs.answer: To resubmit or modify a previously run job, click on the job script under the 'Script Name' column and then click 'Load Parameters'. This will take you back to the job submission page.

qa_pairs.question: What types of applications are supported by Open Composer?
qa_pairs.answer: Open Composer currently supports MPI Slurm Job, OpenMP Slurm Job, Hybrid MPI/OpenMP Slurm Job, Python Slurm Job, R Slurm Job, and VASP Slurm Job.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find an overview of all my jobs in the queue?
qa_pairs.answer: To get an overview of all your jobs in the queue, navigate to the 'Jobs' tab and select 'Active Jobs'.

qa_pairs.question: How can I sort the columns in the general job monitoring interface?
qa_pairs.answer: Columns can be sorted by clicking on the column headers, for example, to sort by job status (running, completed, failed, etc.).

qa_pairs.question: What details can I view by clicking the '>' symbol next to a job in the 'Active Jobs' list?
qa_pairs.answer: Clicking on '>' to the left of a job will show you more details about the job, such as the start/end time, node list, and account charged.

qa_pairs.question: How do I view all jobs in the queue, not just active ones?
qa_pairs.answer: You can show all jobs in the queue by clicking on the drop-down menu at the top right and selecting 'All Jobs'.

qa_pairs.question: Is there another portal for a more detailed view of jobs?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, a more detailed view of your jobs can still be found using the my.SciNet portal.
